content: Vaccines work by teaching your immune system to recognize and fight specific germs without making you sick. When you get a vaccine, it usually contains weakened or inactive parts of a germ, or a blueprint to make those parts, which don’t cause illness. Your immune system spots these harmless parts as invaders and starts building defenses like antibodies to fight them. If the real germ appears later, your immune system recognizes it quickly and uses those pre-made antibodies to stop it before it can cause serious illness. Vaccines significantly reduce the chance of severe illness or complications, even if they don’t always prevent every infection. Serious side effects are very rare because vaccines are carefully tested for safety before approval. Scientists run multiple studies with thousands of volunteers to monitor how vaccines work and identify any side effects before they are widely used. In this way, vaccines safely train your immune system to respond faster and stronger, helping protect you and those around you.
